questions.question:
Draw the precedence graph for the concurrent program given below:

S1
parbegin
  begin S2; S4 end;
  begin
    S3;
    parbegin
      S5;
      S6
    parend;
    S7
  end
parend;
S8

Which of the following precedence graphs correctly represents the above program?
A. S1 -> S2 -> S4, S1 -> S3 -> S5 -> S7 -> S8, S3 -> S6 -> S7, S4 -> S8
B. S1 -> S2 -> S4 -> S8, S1 -> S3 -> S5 -> S7 -> S8, S1 -> S6 -> S7
C. S1 -> S2 -> S4, S1 -> S3, S3 -> S5, S3 -> S6, S5 -> S7, S6 -> S7, S4 -> S8, S7 -> S8
D. S1 -> S2, S1 -> S3, S2 -> S4, S3 -> S5, S3 -> S6, S4 -> S8, S5 -> S8, S6 -> S8
questions.to_find: The precedence graph representing the execution order of statements in the concurrent program

understand.plain: A concurrent program uses parbegin/parend to indicate which statements can run in parallel. We need to draw a directed acyclic graph (precedence graph) showing which statements must complete before others can start. Statements inside the same parbegin/parend block run concurrently, but statements in sequence must respect their order.

keywords.explain: Delimiters that mark the start and end of a parallel section; all processes/statements within run concurrently.
keywords.example: parbegin S2; S3 parend means S2 and S3 run simultaneously.

keywords.explain: A directed acyclic graph (DAG) where an edge S_i -> S_j means S_i must finish before S_j begins.
keywords.example: S1 -> S2 means S1 must complete before S2 starts.

keywords.explain: Statements written one after another (begin S2; S4 end) must execute in that order, creating a chain in the precedence graph.
keywords.example: begin S2; S4 end yields edge S2 -> S4.

class='sub mut'>S8 (after S4 and S7)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Precedence graph: S1 at top. S1 -> S2 -> S4 (left branch). S1 -> S3 -> S5 and S3 -> S6 (parallel), then S5 -> S7 and S6 -> S7. Finally S4 -> S8 and S7 -> S8.

given.aim: Construct the precedence graph that captures the execution dependencies in the concurrent program.

terms.meaning: S1 must complete before any of the outer parallel branches start.
terms.connects: S1 is the root of the graph.

terms.meaning: Branch 1: sequential 'begin S2; S4 end' (S2 -> S4). Branch 2: 'begin S3; parbegin S5; S6 parend; S7 end' (S3 -> S5, S3 -> S6, S5 -> S7, S6 -> S7).
terms.example: S2 must finish before S4; S3 before S5 and S6; both S5 and S6 before S7.
terms.connects: Determines all edges within the two parallel branches.

terms.meaning: S8 executes after the outer parbegin/parend finishes, i.e., after both branch 1 (S4) and branch 2 (S7) complete.
terms.connects: S8 is the final node of the graph.

given.plan: Trace the program structure: sequential statements create chain edges, parbegin/parend creates fan-out from the preceding statement and fan-in to the subsequent statement. Build the DAG step by step.

steps.title: Identify top-level structure
steps.apply: S1 executes sequentially first. Then the outer parbegin starts two concurrent branches. After both branches complete (parend), S8 executes. So edges: S1 -> (start of each branch) and (end of each branch) -> S8.
steps.note: The outer parbegin has exactly two branches.

steps.title: Analyze Branch 1: begin S2; S4 end
steps.apply: This is a sequential block: S1 -> S2 -> S4. Since branch 1 ends at S4, we get S4 -> S8.
steps.note: Sequential begin...end within parbegin means the steps inside are ordered.

steps.title: Analyze Branch 2: begin S3; parbegin S5; S6 parend; S7 end
steps.apply: S3 executes first in this branch (S1 -> S3). Then the inner parbegin makes S5 and S6 concurrent: S3 -> S5 and S3 -> S6. After both finish (inner parend), S7 runs: S5 -> S7 and S6 -> S7. Branch 2 ends at S7, so S7 -> S8.
steps.note: Nested parbegin/parend creates an inner parallel section within branch 2.

steps.title: Compile full edge set
steps.apply: Edges: S1->S2, S2->S4, S1->S3, S3->S5, S3->S6, S5->S7, S6->S7, S4->S8, S7->S8. This matches option C.
steps.note: Option C correctly captures all sequential and parallel dependencies.

questions.formula_note: Precedence graph construction rules: sequential statements create a chain; parbegin/parend creates fan-out from predecessor and fan-in to successor.

questions.question:
The process state transition diagram in the below figure is representative of

(The diagram shows states: Running, Ready, Blocked, and transitions including: Running -> Ready (preemption), Ready -> Running (dispatch), Running -> Blocked (I/O wait), Blocked -> Ready (I/O complete), and notably NO direct path from Running to Exit/Terminated)

A. a batch operating system
B. an operating system with a preemptive scheduler
C. an operating system with a non-preemptive scheduler
D. a uni-programmed operating system
questions.answer: B. an operating system with a preemptive scheduler
questions.to_find: What type of operating system the given process state transition diagram represents

understand.plain: A process state diagram shows the states a process can be in (Running, Ready, Blocked) and the events that cause transitions between them. The key distinguishing transition is whether a running process can be forcibly moved back to the Ready state — this is called preemption. If there is an arrow from Running to Ready (i.e., the OS can kick a running process off the CPU), the scheduler is preemptive.

keywords.explain: A scheduler that can forcibly remove a running process from the CPU and put it back in the Ready queue, allowing another process to run.
keywords.example: Time-slice expiry in Round Robin causes Running -> Ready transition.

keywords.explain: A scheduler where a process runs until it voluntarily gives up the CPU (by blocking on I/O or terminating). There is NO Running -> Ready transition.
keywords.example: FCFS is non-preemptive: a process runs to completion or until it blocks.

keywords.explain: An event-driven change in a process's state (Running, Ready, Blocked, New, Terminated).
keywords.example: A process doing I/O transitions from Running to Blocked.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='480' height='220' viewBox='0 0 480 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><defs><marker id='a' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#2C2C2A'/></marker></defs><rect x='190' y='20' width='100' height='44' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='240' y='47' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Running</text><rect x='30' y='130' width='100' height='44' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='157' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Ready</text><rect x='350' y='130' width='100' height='44' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='400' y='157' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Blocked</text><line x1='190' y1='42' x2='130' y2='130' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5' marker-end='url(#a)'/><text x='140' y='90' class='sub ink'>Preempt</text><line x1='130' y1='150' x2='190' y2='55' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2.5' marker-end='url(#a)'/><text x='138' y='120' class='sub ink'>Dispatch</text><line x1='290' y1='42' x2='355' y2='130' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#a)'/><text x='330' y='85' class='sub ink'>I/O wait</text><line x1='352' y1='152' x2='132' y2='152' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#a)'/><text x='220' y='148' class='sub ink'>I/O done</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: State diagram with Running, Ready, Blocked. Arrows: Running->Ready (preemption), Ready->Running (dispatch), Running->Blocked (I/O wait), Blocked->Ready (I/O complete). The Running->Ready arrow indicates preemptive scheduling.

given.aim: Identify the type of OS from the presence of a Running -> Ready transition in the state diagram.

terms.meaning: This transition occurs when the OS forcibly removes a process from the CPU (preemption).
terms.example: Timer interrupt causes a context switch, moving current process to Ready queue.
terms.connects: The presence of this arrow is the defining characteristic of a preemptive scheduler.

terms.meaning: Process voluntarily waits for I/O or an event.
terms.example: read() system call blocks the process.
terms.connects: This exists in both preemptive and non-preemptive systems.

terms.meaning: The awaited event (I/O completion) has occurred, so the process becomes eligible to run again.
terms.example: Disk read completes, interrupt handler moves process to Ready queue.

given.plan: Check whether the diagram includes a Running -> Ready transition (preemption). If yes, the scheduler is preemptive. Eliminate other options based on this key observation.

steps.title: Identify the key transition in the diagram
steps.apply: The diagram shows a transition from Running directly back to Ready state. This transition is labeled as 'preemption' or 'time-slice expiry' — meaning the OS can forcibly take the CPU away from a running process.
steps.note: This transition is absent in non-preemptive schedulers.

steps.title: Eliminate incorrect options
steps.apply: A: Batch OS can be either preemptive or non-preemptive — not specific enough. C: Non-preemptive scheduler would NOT have a Running->Ready transition. D: Uni-programmed OS has only one process at a time, so Ready state is unnecessary. Option B (preemptive scheduler) is the only one consistent with a Running->Ready transition.
steps.note: The Running->Ready arrow is the hallmark of preemptive scheduling.

solution.result: B. an operating system with a preemptive scheduler

questions.formula_note: Process state diagram analysis: Running -> Ready transition is unique to preemptive scheduling systems.

questions.question:
Which of the following does NOT interrupt a running process?

A. A device
B. Timer
C. Scheduler process
D. Power failure
questions.to_find: Which entity among the given options does NOT cause an interrupt on a running process

understand.plain: Interrupts are hardware or software signals that cause the CPU to stop the currently running process and transfer control to the OS. Devices generate hardware interrupts (e.g., I/O completion), timers generate timer interrupts (used for time-sharing), and power failures generate interrupts. A 'scheduler process' is not a real entity that generates interrupts — the scheduler is part of the OS and runs only when the CPU is already with the OS (during interrupt handling or system calls), not as a separate interrupt source.

keywords.explain: A signal from a hardware device to the CPU indicating it needs attention, causing the current process to be interrupted.
keywords.example: Disk I/O completion sends an interrupt to notify the CPU.

keywords.explain: A periodic interrupt generated by a hardware timer chip, used by the OS to preempt running processes for time-sharing.
keywords.example: Every 10ms a timer fires, giving the OS a chance to run the scheduler.

keywords.explain: The OS component that decides which process runs next. It does NOT generate interrupts — it is invoked BY interrupt handlers.
keywords.example: After a timer interrupt, the interrupt handler calls the scheduler to pick the next process.

understand.visual_alt: Table of interrupt sources: Device (yes - hardware IRQ), Timer (yes - timer IRQ), Scheduler process (no - not an interrupt source), Power failure (yes - NMI or system shutdown signal).

given.aim: Identify which of the four options is NOT a source of interrupts for a running process.

terms.meaning: I/O devices (disk, keyboard, NIC) raise hardware interrupts when they need CPU attention.
A hardware timer generates periodic interrupts used by the OS for preemptive multitasking.
terms.example: Round-Robin scheduling relies on timer interrupts.
terms.connects: Directly interrupts a running process to enable time-sharing.

terms.meaning: A power supply failure generates a Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI) or triggers system shutdown, interrupting any running process.
terms.example: UPS systems detect power failure and generate an NMI.
terms.connects: Interrupts running process for emergency handling.

given.plan: Check each option: devices, timers, and power failures are hardware interrupt sources. The scheduler is OS software that runs after an interrupt — it is not itself an interrupt source.

steps.title: Check each option as an interrupt source
steps.apply: A. A device: YES — I/O devices generate hardware interrupts (IRQ lines) that interrupt the running process. B. Timer: YES — the timer chip periodically fires interrupts used for preemptive scheduling. D. Power failure: YES — generates a Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI) or equivalent signal. C. Scheduler process: NO — the scheduler is a part of the OS kernel that is invoked AFTER an interrupt occurs; it is not itself an interrupt source.
steps.note: The scheduler is invoked during interrupt service routines, not as an independent interrupt generator.

steps.title: Confirm option C as the answer
steps.apply: The 'scheduler process' is a conceptual OS component, not a hardware interrupt source. It cannot interrupt a running process on its own — it only runs when control is already in the OS (e.g., after a timer interrupt fires). Therefore, the scheduler process does NOT interrupt a running process.
steps.note: Some might confuse 'scheduler runs' with 'scheduler interrupts' but the OS scheduler executes within existing interrupt/trap context.

questions.formula_note: Interrupt sources are hardware-generated signals (device IRQ, timer, NMI for power failure). The OS scheduler is software invoked by interrupt handlers, not an interrupt source itself.

questions.question:
Which combination of the following features will suffice to characterize an OS as a multi-programmed OS?

a. More than one program may be loaded into main memory at the same time for execution.
b. If a program waits for certain events such as I/O, another program is immediately scheduled for execution.
c. If the execution of a program terminates, another program is immediately scheduled for execution.

A. (a)
B. (a) and (b)
C. (a) and (c)
D. (a), (b) and (c)
questions.to_find: The minimum set of features that characterize a multi-programmed operating system

understand.plain: Multiprogramming means keeping multiple programs in memory at the same time and switching between them to keep the CPU busy. The essential idea is: when one program is waiting (e.g., for I/O), the OS picks another program and runs it. Feature (a) is necessary — you need multiple programs in memory. Feature (b) is necessary — CPU switches to another program when current one waits. Feature (c) (switching on termination only) is a special case of scheduling but is also present in a simple batch system, not uniquely multiprogramming. The key distinction of multiprogramming is (a) + (b).

keywords.explain: The technique of keeping multiple programs in memory simultaneously so the CPU can switch to another when the current one waits for I/O, maximizing CPU utilization.
keywords.example: While program A waits for disk I/O, the CPU runs program B.

keywords.explain: The fraction of time the CPU is busy doing useful work. Multiprogramming improves CPU utilization by avoiding idle time during I/O waits.
keywords.example: Without multiprogramming, CPU idles during all I/O operations.

keywords.explain: A simple batch OS runs one job at a time; a multiprogrammed OS overlaps execution of multiple jobs to keep CPU busy.
keywords.example: Multiprogrammed OS: CPU switches from job A (doing I/O) to job B automatically.

understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram or timeline showing: In multiprogramming, while Job A waits for I/O, Job B uses the CPU. Both jobs are in memory simultaneously (feature a) and the switch happens on I/O wait (feature b).

given.aim: Determine the minimal set of features (a), (b), (c) that defines multiprogramming.

terms.meaning: Multiple programs loaded in RAM simultaneously, ready to be executed.
terms.example: Programs P1, P2, P3 all reside in memory at the same time.
terms.connects: Necessary condition — without this, only one program exists and multiprogramming is impossible.

terms.meaning: When a running program blocks on I/O, another program is scheduled immediately.
terms.example: P1 issues a disk read; OS switches CPU to P2 while disk works.
terms.connects: This is the defining behavior of multiprogramming — CPU never idles due to I/O.

terms.meaning: When a program finishes, another program is scheduled.
terms.example: Batch processing also does this — it is not unique to multiprogramming.
terms.connects: Feature (c) alone (with a) describes simple batch processing, not full multiprogramming.

given.plan: Feature (a) is necessary for multiple programs. Feature (b) is the key multiprogramming behavior (CPU utilization during I/O). Feature (c) is a weaker condition present in simple batch systems. Multiprogramming requires (a) + (b); (c) alone with (a) is insufficient to characterize true multiprogramming.

steps.title: Evaluate feature (a) alone
steps.apply: Having multiple programs in memory alone does NOT guarantee the CPU stays busy. Without a policy to switch when one blocks on I/O, only one runs at a time despite all being in memory. So (a) alone is insufficient — eliminates option A.
steps.note: Feature (a) is necessary but not sufficient.

steps.title: Evaluate (a) + (b) vs (a) + (c)
steps.apply: (a) + (b): Multiple programs in memory AND CPU switches to another when current one waits for I/O. This IS multiprogramming — the CPU is kept busy by exploiting I/O wait periods. (a) + (c): Multiple programs in memory AND switching only on termination — this is essentially a batch system where only one program runs at a time to completion. This does NOT capture the I/O-overlap benefit of multiprogramming.
steps.note: The core benefit of multiprogramming is CPU-I/O overlap, captured by feature (b).

steps.title: Evaluate (a) + (b) + (c)
steps.apply: (a) + (b) + (c) is also a valid multiprogrammed system, but (c) is redundant — it is implied by any reasonable scheduler. The minimal characterizing set is (a) + (b). Among the options, B '(a) and (b)' is the correct answer as the sufficient and defining combination.
steps.note: Adding (c) doesn't hurt but is not the distinguishing characteristic; (a)+(b) is the standard definition.

questions.formula_note: Multiprogramming definition: multiple programs in memory (a) + CPU switches on I/O wait (b). This maximizes CPU utilization through I/O-CPU overlap.
